none: Environmental Impact Statement, The Construction of the Second Avenue Subway in New York City, New York, 34th Street to 126th Street, Manhattan

The Second Avenue Subway project in New York City aims to construct a 4.7-mile subway line from 34th Street to 126th Street in Manhattan. This project is intended to alleviate overcrowding on the Lexington Avenue Line, providing a new transit option on the east side of Manhattan. It promises to improve public transportation, reduce surface traffic congestion, and decrease vehicular emissions. While the construction will cause temporary disruptions, such as noise and traffic interference, the long-term benefits include enhanced transit capacity and connectivity, contributing to the city's economic vitality.
Environmental Impact Statement, The Construction of the Second Avenue Subway in New York City, New York, 34th Street to 126th Street, Manhattan

The Second Avenue Subway project in New York City aims to construct a 4.7-mile subway line from 34th Street to 126th Street in Manhattan. This project is intended to alleviate overcrowding on the Lexington Avenue Line, providing a new transit option on the east side of Manhattan. It promises to improve public transportation, reduce surface traffic congestion, and decrease vehicular emissions. While the construction will cause temporary disruptions, such as noise and traffic interference, the long-term benefits include enhanced transit capacity and connectivity, contributing to the city's economic vitality.
Manhattan, New York City, New York
Council on the Environment of New York City

The Second Avenue Subway project was proposed to address the chronic overcrowding on the Lexington Avenue Line, which has long been a critical transit route for Manhattan's east side. The project faced significant public pushback due to concerns over construction disruptions and the project's lengthy timeline, which spanned several decades before significant progress was made. Ultimately, the first phase of the subway was completed and opened in January 2017, with plans for future expansions still being discussed.

"The Committee has recognized the existing New York City subway system to be one of the City's most serious noise sources -- a conclusion reached independently by most City residents." — Council on the Environment of New York City
"The Second Avenue Line will be much quieter than existing lines in the New York Transit System." — Public Comments – Section 5.2
